title: New and Old Observations About Morrey's Quasi-Convexity
speaker: Vladimir Sverak
abstract: The notion of singular support has its origin in the theory of partial differential equations, and was introduced to the world of constructible sheaves by M. Kashiwara and P. Schapira in the 1970s. It is a basic invariant (like the support), and, together with the characteristic cycle, has applications in many areas, including partial differential equations, geometric Langlands, and exponential sums. In this talk, I will discuss the singular support in three contexts: complex analytic spaces (Kashiwara and Schapira), algebraic varieties (A. Beilinson, T. Saito), and rigid analytic varieties (in progress).

proof_stages.label: Morrey quasiconvexity and its standard convexity relatives
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up vectorial variational integrals, states Morrey quasiconvexity as affine minimality and weak lower semicontinuity, then reviews rank-one convexity as a necessary ellipticity condition and polyconvexity via null Lagrangians as a sufficient condition.

proof_stages.label: The rank-one-convex versus quasiconvex problem and the Burkholder test integrand
proof_stages.summary: The central open question is formulated: whether the Legendre-Hadamard/rank-one convexity condition implies quasiconvexity in the difficult low-dimensional cases, with a specific two-by-two Burkholder-type function presented as the most important unresolved test.

proof_stages.label: Radial tests and second variation near Burkholder zeroes
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that rank-one convexity already yields the quasiconvexity inequality on radial deformations, often with exact equality for the Burkholder function, and that a second-variation computation gives positivity rather than a nearby counterexample.

proof_stages.label: Universal deformations producing high-codimension counterexamples
proof_stages.summary: To explain known failures of rank-one convexity implying quasiconvexity in higher target dimension, the talk constructs universal finite-dimensional deformations through which all tests factor, converts the rank-one geometry into separate convexity in complex coordinates, and shows why averaging over embedded low-dimensional tori can fail.

proof_stages.label: Null-Lagrangian closure, elliptic regularity failures, and singularity heuristics
proof_stages.summary: The universal-deformation geometry is combined with null Lagrangians to build polyconvex/coercive augmentations giving elliptic systems with bad regularity, after which known and conjectural singularities are interpreted through the same universal-deformation lens.

proof_stages.label: Linear-programming approach to laminates and computational searches
proof_stages.summary: The recent approach recasts the search for a quasiconvexity counterexample as minimization of linear functionals over the cone of rank-one convex functions or laminates, studies obstructions to compactness, and replaces the infinite problem by finite polynomial LP relaxations suitable for computation.
